# Designing Data-Intensive Applications, Second Edition (Martin Kleppmann & Chris Riccomini)

Before writing, modifying, or planning any code, you MUST use your `read_file` tool to load the relevant guidelines from `.roo/vendor/`. You may read multiple files if the task crosses domains.

## Table of Contents:
    - [Trade-offs in Data Systems Architecture](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/01-trade-offs-in-data-systems-architecture.md): System architecture design, data infrastructure setup, database selection, data pipeline creation (ETL/ELT), cloud infrastructure provisioning, microservice boundary definition, performance/cost optimization, and privacy/compliance planning.
    - [Defining Nonfunctional Requirements](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/02-defining-nonfunctional-requirements.md): Designing system architecture, evaluating or implementing performance metrics, defining Service Level Objectives (SLOs) or Service Level Agreements (SLAs), planning system capacity and scalability, designing fault-tolerance mechanisms, writing system incident reports, or defining nonfunctional requirements for a software application.
    - [Data Models and Query Languages](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/03-data-models-and-query-languages.md): Database architecture, data modeling, schema design, database migrations, query optimization, API data payload design, or evaluating the trade-offs between different database paradigms (e.g., Relational, NoSQL/Document, Graph, Event Sourcing, Dataframes).
    - [Storage and Retrieval](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/04-storage-and-retrieval.md): Database architecture design, storage engine selection, data modeling for performance, index optimization, query tuning, data warehouse design, or the implementation of advanced search systems (full-text, spatial, or semantic vector search).
    - [Encoding and Evolution](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/05-encoding-and-evolution.md): The AI is tasked with defining, modifying, or evaluating data models, schemas, serialization/encoding formats (e.g., JSON, Protocol Buffers, Avro), API designs (REST, gRPC, RPC), workflow definitions (durable execution), or asynchronous message-passing architectures (message brokers, actor models).
    - [Replication](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/06-replication.md): Designing, analyzing, configuring, or troubleshooting distributed data systems, database architectures, state synchronization, multi-region deployments, offline-first applications, collaborative software, or any system requiring data to be copied and maintained across multiple network-connected nodes.
    - [Sharding](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/07-sharding.md): Designing, architecting, optimizing, or refactoring distributed database systems, data storage layers, horizontally scalable systems, multi-tenant SaaS data stores, or resolving database scalability, query routing, or partitioning challenges.
    - [Transactions](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/08-transactions.md): Writing database access code, designing database schemas, implementing Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) logic, writing concurrent data manipulation logic, configuring database isolation levels, and handling error/retry logic for data persistence operations.
    - [The Trouble with Distributed Systems](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/09-the-trouble-with-distributed-systems.md): Architecture design, code review, debugging, or implementation of distributed systems, microservices, network communication (RPC/REST APIs), data replication, or fault-tolerant infrastructure.
    - [Consistency and Consensus](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/10-consistency-and-consensus.md): distributed systems coordination, leader election, distributed locking, distributed transactions, strict ordering of operations, cross-node consistency models, or the resolution of split-brain and network partition scenarios.
    - [Batch Processing](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/11-batch-processing.md): Large-scale offline data transformations, ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines, distributed joins, MapReduce algorithms, dataflow engine configurations (e.g., Apache Spark, Apache Flink in batch mode), and bulk data processing systems.
    - [Stream Processing](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/12-stream-processing.md): Any task, file, or user request involving Stream Processing, Event Sourcing, Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS), or Event-Driven Architectures (Message Brokers/Actor Frameworks).
    - [Doing the Right Thing](.roo/vendor/architecture/designing-data-intensive-applications-second-edition-martin-kleppmann-chris-riccomini/13-doing-the-right-thing.md): Designing, architecting, reviewing, or writing code for data storage systems, event logs, analytics pipelines, machine learning feature engineering, or user-tracking mechanisms, including tasks involving data collection schemas, database migrations, data retention policies, algorithmic decision-making systems, and integration with payment or external vendor systems.
